name: Rangi
description: Rangi, also known as Ranginui, is the Sky Father in Māori mythology. He is a primordial being who, along with Papa, the Earth Mother, formed the foundation of the universe. Initially, Rangi and Papa were locked in a tight embrace, their children trapped in the darkness between them. This oppressive darkness spurred their children, the gods, to seek separation. After many attempts, Tāne Mahuta, god of the forests, successfully forced his parents apart, pushing Rangi upwards to become the sky and allowing light to flood the world. Rangi's tears are said to be the rain, a constant reminder of his separation from Papa. He represents the heavens, the realm of light, and the source of knowledge and spiritual power. His separation from Papa is a central theme in Māori creation narratives, symbolizing the emergence of the world from darkness and the beginning of life. Rangi's influence is felt in the weather, the stars, and the connection between the earthly and spiritual realms. He is often invoked in prayers and ceremonies, representing the ancestral lineage and the ultimate source of being. He is a powerful and revered figure, embodying the sky's vastness and the mysteries of the cosmos. His separation from Papa also birthed the different realms of existence, with the gods each taking responsibility over different aspects of the world. Rangi's enduring sadness and the constant rain are a poignant reminder of the sacrifice made to bring light and life to the world.
subtitle: Sky Father, Source of Light and Rain

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Rangi and Papa were locked in a tight embrace, trapping their children in darkness. Tāne Mahuta pushed them apart, creating the world of light.

keyMyths.title: Rangi's Tears
keyMyths.description: After being separated from Papa, Rangi weeps constantly, his tears falling as rain upon the earth. This rain sustains life and connects the sky and earth.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend

keyMyths.title: The Children's Struggle
keyMyths.description: The children of Rangi and Papa struggled in the darkness, seeking a way to bring light to the world. Their collective efforts led to the separation.
keyMyths.source: Reed, Māori Myths and Legendary Tales

sources.author: Sir George Grey

sources.text: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends, providing foundational accounts of Rangi and Papa.

cultural.modernLegacy: Rangi remains a significant figure in Māori culture, representing the sky and the connection to the heavens. He is often invoked in karakia (prayers) and stories, and his separation from Papa continues to be a powerful metaphor for creation and change. Contemporary Māori art and literature often feature Rangi and Papa, highlighting their enduring importance in cultural identity and storytelling. The narrative of Rangi and Papa is used to teach about balance, relationships, and the origins of the world.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, according to Māori tradition, there was only Te Kore, the Void, a state of nothingness from which all things would eventually emerge. From this void came Te Pō, the Darkness, a period of gestation and potential. Within Te Pō, Rangi (Ranginui, the Sky Father) and Papa (Papatūānuku, the Earth Mother) came into being. They existed in a close, loving embrace, locked together in perpetual darkness. Their offspring, the gods, were trapped between them, in the cramped and lightless space. This primordial state represented the beginning of existence but also a state of stagnation, as the potential for life and growth was stifled by the tight embrace of the Sky Father and Earth Mother. The desire of their children to experience light and freedom would ultimately lead to the separation of Rangi and Papa, marking a pivotal moment in Māori cosmology.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth involving Rangi revolves around his separation from Papa. Trapped in darkness between their parents, the children of Rangi and Papa grew weary of their confinement. They plotted to separate their parents to bring light and space into the world. Each sibling tried in turn, but it was Tāne Mahuta, god of the forest, who succeeded. By bracing himself against Papa and pushing upwards with his legs against Rangi, Tāne forced the Sky Father and Earth Mother apart. This act brought light and air into the world, allowing life to flourish. Rangi, now separated from his beloved Papa, wept tears that formed the rain. He continues to grieve for his lost love, explaining the ever-present connection between the sky and the earth. The separation is not portrayed as an act of hatred, but as a necessary sacrifice for the sake of creation and the well-being of their offspring.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Rangi's legacy is deeply woven into the fabric of Māori culture and worldview. As the Sky Father, he represents the heavens, the realm of the gods, and the source of rain and other celestial phenomena. His separation from Papa is a foundational story that explains the origin of the world and the interconnectedness of all things. The tears of Rangi, falling as rain, nourish Papa and allow life to thrive, symbolizing the ongoing relationship between the sky and the earth. In contemporary Māori society, Rangi continues to be a powerful symbol of heritage and identity. His story is often used to teach about the importance of balance, respect for the environment, and the enduring strength of familial bonds. The themes of creation, separation, and reconciliation found in the myth of Rangi and Papa resonate deeply with contemporary Māori experiences.

shortDescription: Rangi, or Ranginui, is the Sky Father in Māori mythology. He is a primordial deity who, with Papa, the Earth Mother, forms the foundation of the universe.
